knowledge.what_it_is: The internet is a global network of interconnected computers and servers that communicate with each other using standardized protocols, enabling data exchange between devices worldwide.
what_you_will_learn: TCP/IP model — Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol stack: 4 layers (Application, Transport, Internet, Link) responsible for routing packets across the internet
IP addressing — IPv4 and IPv6 address formats, subnetting, CIDR notation, and IP routing protocols like OSPF and BGP
DNS resolution — Domain Name System: translating human-readable domain names to IP addresses using recursive resolvers and caching mechanisms
HTTP/HTTPS protocols — Hypertext Transfer Protocol/Secure: request-response model for web communication, including methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), headers, and status codes
Packet switching — how data is fragmented into packets, routed through networks, and reassembled at the destination using routing tables and hop-by-hop forwarding
Network topologies — physical and logical network layouts, including star, bus, ring, mesh, and hybrid configurations
Firewalls and NAT — Network Address Translation: configuring firewalls to control incoming/outgoing traffic, hiding internal IP addresses from external networks

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is a request-response protocol used for data communication between client and server applications over the internet. It's a standardized way to exchange data, including web pages, images, videos, and more, using TCP/IP as its underlying transport mechanism.
what_you_will_learn: Request Methods — HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD) used for different operations on resources
HTTP Status Codes — understanding the meaning of status codes like 200 OK, 404 Not Found, and 500 Internal Server Error
HTTP Headers — learning about common headers like Host, Accept, Content-Type, and Authorization
Request Body — understanding how to send data in the request body using methods like POST and PUT
Response Body — learning how to handle server responses, including parsing JSON and XML data
HTTP Caching — understanding how browsers and servers cache resources for faster page loads
HTTP Authentication — learning about basic authentication (username/password) and more secure alternatives like OAuth

knowledge.what_it_is: A domain name is a human-readable identifier that maps to an IP address, allowing users to access websites and online services using a memorable and easy-to-type string. In the context of backend development, understanding domain names is crucial for building scalable and secure web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Domain Name System (DNS) — a hierarchical system that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses
Fully Qualified Domain Names (FQDNs) — the full name of a domain, including its subdomains and top-level domains
Top-Level Domains (TLDs) — the highest level of domain hierarchy, such as .com, .org, or country-code TLDs like .us or .uk
Subdomains — nested levels within a domain, often used for organization or departmental separation
Domain Name Registration — the process of reserving a unique domain name with a registrar, such as GoDaddy or Namecheap
DNS Record Types — various types of records that define how DNS resolves domain names, including A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, and TXT records

knowledge.what_it_is: Hosting refers to the process of storing and serving web applications, websites, or services on remote servers over the internet. In a backend development context, hosting involves deploying code changes, managing server resources, and ensuring application availability.
what_you_will_learn: Server types — understanding the differences between IaaS (AWS EC2), PaaS (Heroku), CaaS (Docker Swarm) and FaaS (AWS Lambda)
Cloud providers — familiarizing with major cloud hosting platforms: AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, DigitalOcean, Linode
Containerization — using Docker to package applications and their dependencies into containers for efficient deployment
Orchestration tools — learning about Kubernetes, Apache Mesos, or other container orchestration systems
Serverless computing — understanding the benefits and trade-offs of serverless architectures with AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, or Azure Functions
Load balancing — configuring load balancers (HAProxy, NGINX) to distribute traffic across multiple servers for improved scalability
Caching mechanisms — implementing caching strategies using Redis, Memcached, or in-memory data grids like Hazelcast
Monitoring and logging — setting up monitoring tools (Prometheus, Grafana) and logging frameworks (ELK Stack, Splunk) for application performance analysis

knowledge.what_it_is: DNS (Domain Name System) is a hierarchical distributed database that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses, enabling communication between devices on the internet. It's a critical component of the internet infrastructure and a fundamental concept for backend developers to understand.
what_you_will_learn: DNS resolution — the process of converting domain names to IP addresses using recursive resolvers or caching DNS servers
DNS record types (A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, SOA) — understanding the different types of records and their purposes in the DNS system
Zone files — managing DNS records for a specific domain or zone using text files or DNS management tools like PowerDNS or Bind
DNS propagation — understanding how changes to DNS records are propagated across the internet and affecting website availability
DNS caching — implementing caching mechanisms on servers or clients to improve DNS resolution performance and reduce latency
DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions) — securing DNS transactions using digital signatures and public-key cryptography
DNS over HTTPS (DoH) and DNS over TLS (DoT) — understanding the benefits and implementation of encrypted DNS protocols for improved security and privacy

knowledge.what_it_is: A web browser is an application that renders HTML, CSS, and JavaScript on a client-side device to display web pages. It's crucial for backend developers to understand how browsers work to optimize their applications' performance, security, and user experience.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP Request-Response Cycle — understanding the sequence of events between a browser sending an HTTP request and receiving a response from a server
Browser Rendering Engine — learning about the different rendering engines (e.g., Blink, Gecko, EdgeHTML) and their role in parsing HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
DOM and Document Object Model — understanding how browsers create a DOM representation of the web page and manipulate it using JavaScript
Event Loop and Task Queue — grasping how browsers manage asynchronous tasks, event handling, and callback functions
Cache Management — learning about browser caching mechanisms (e.g., Service Worker, Cache API) to improve performance and reduce latency
Security Features — understanding browser security features such as Same-Origin Policy, CORS, and Content Security Policy
Browser Storage and Cookies — knowing how browsers store data locally using cookies, local storage, and session storage

knowledge.what_it_is: PHP (Hypertext Preprocessor) is a mature, open-source scripting language used for web development, especially for server-side logic, database interactions, and dynamic content generation. It's widely used for building scalable, secure, and maintainable web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — declaring and using variables in PHP, including type juggling and coercion
Data Types — understanding PHP's data types (int, float, string, array, object, null), including implicit conversions
Operators — mastering PHP's operators for arithmetic, comparison, logical, assignment, and increment/decrement
Control Structures — using if/else statements, switch statements, loops (for, while, foreach), and jump statements (break, continue)
Functions — defining and calling functions in PHP, including arguments, return types, and variable scope
Arrays — working with arrays in PHP, including array operations, multidimensional arrays, and array filtering
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) — understanding PHP's OOP concepts (classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism)
Exception Handling — using try/catch blocks to handle exceptions, including custom exception classes and error reporting
File Input/Output — reading and writing files in PHP, including file modes, permissions, and buffering
Database Interactions — connecting to databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite) using PDO or MySQLi extensions
Security Best Practices — understanding PHP's security features (e.g., prepared statements, input validation)
Error Handling — configuring error reporting, logging errors, and debugging techniques
Caching Mechanisms — implementing caching with PHP (e.g., APCu, Memcached, Redis)
Dependency Injection — using dependency injection containers in PHP (e.g., Laravel's IoC container)
Authentication and Authorization — implementing authentication and authorization mechanisms in PHP (e.g., sessions, JWT)
API Design Principles — designing RESTful APIs with PHP, including API endpoints, request/response formats, and error handling
Testing Frameworks — using testing frameworks like PHPUnit or Pest to write unit tests and integration tests for PHP code
Code Quality Tools — using tools like PHP_CodeSniffer, PHPMD, or Psalm to improve code quality and maintainability

knowledge.what_it_is: Rust is a systems programming language that focuses on memory safety and performance, designed to replace C++ as a go-to choice for building low-level system software. It's statically typed, compiled, and garbage-collected, with a strong focus on concurrency and parallelism.
what_you_will_learn: Ownership — Rust's core concept: managing memory through borrowing and ownership
Borrowing — understanding the different types of borrows (immutable, mutable, shared)
Lifetimes — specifying the duration for which a value is valid in memory
Smart pointers — using Box, Rc, Arc, and Mutex to manage complex data structures
Error handling — using Result and Option to handle errors in a safe way
Traits — defining interfaces for types to implement specific behaviors
Modules — organizing code into reusable packages with clear dependencies
Crate ecosystem — understanding how to use Cargo, the Rust package manager
Rust standard library — exploring the built-in functions and data structures
Async/await — writing asynchronous code using async/await syntax
Tokio — a popular async runtime for building concurrent systems
async-std — another popular async runtime with a focus on performance
Rust's concurrency model — understanding how to write safe concurrent code
Mutex and RwLock — synchronizing access to shared resources using locks
Condvars and Semaphores — using synchronization primitives for inter-thread communication
Rust's borrow checker — understanding the rules for memory safety in Rust
Pattern matching — using match statements to handle different values
Generics — writing reusable code with type parameters
Type inference — letting the compiler infer types automatically
Rust's macro system — using procedural macros and declarative macros
Derive macros — generating boilerplate code for common tasks
Rust's testing framework — using the built-in test module to write unit tests

knowledge.what_it_is: Go, also known as Golang, is an open-source programming language developed by Google. It was designed to be a modern alternative to C++ and provides a clean syntax, strong focus on concurrency, and robust standard library.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — Go's type system and variable declaration syntax
Basic data types — int, float64, string, bool, rune, byte
Arrays and slices — dynamic arrays with built-in methods for manipulation
Maps — key-value stores with efficient lookup and insertion
Structs and interfaces — defining custom data structures and behaviors
Functions — Go's function declaration syntax and return types
Error handling — using errors as values and the error type
Concurrency — goroutines, channels, mutexes for safe concurrent execution
Channels — synchronization primitives for passing data between goroutines
Mutexes — locks for protecting shared resources in concurrent code
Select statement — a way to wait on multiple channels with timeouts
Close and range statements — safely closing channels and iterating over values
Context API — propagating cancellation signals through function calls
HTTP/2 support — building efficient, multiplexed HTTP servers
net/http package — building simple web servers and clients
goroutine scheduling — understanding the Go runtime's threading model
panic and recover — safe error handling with explicit recovery
testing framework — writing unit tests and integration tests for Go code
Go modules — dependency management and versioning for Go projects
gofmt and govet tools — enforcing coding standards and best practices
Go's standard library — a comprehensive collection of packages for common tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: JavaScript is a high-level, dynamic, and interpreted programming language used for both front-end and back-end web development. It's widely supported by most browsers and servers, making it a popular choice for building scalable and maintainable web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Variables and data types — understanding the difference between var, let, const, and their usage in JavaScript
Functions — defining and using functions to encapsulate code reuse and modularization
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts — classes, inheritance, polymorphism, and encapsulation in JavaScript
Async programming — understanding callbacks, promises, async/await, and their application in asynchronous operations
Modules and npm — installing, importing, and exporting modules using CommonJS and ES6 syntax
Node.js fundamentals — setting up a Node.js environment, creating server-side applications, and handling requests
Express.js framework — building RESTful APIs, routing, middleware, and error handling with Express
Database interactions — connecting to MongoDB, MySQL, PostgreSQL, or other databases using Mongoose, Sequelize, or native drivers
Authentication and Authorization — implementing user authentication and authorization using Passport.js, JWT, or OAuth
WebSockets — establishing bidirectional communication between clients and servers for real-time updates
RESTful API design principles — designing scalable, maintainable, and REST-compliant APIs
Error handling and logging — catching and logging errors using try-catch blocks, error handlers, and logging libraries like Winston or Morgan
Security best practices — understanding common web vulnerabilities (XSS, CSRF, SQL injection) and implementing security measures
Deployment strategies — deploying Node.js applications to cloud platforms (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure), containerization with Docker, and continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD)
Performance optimization techniques — improving application performance using caching, minification, compression, and code splitting
TypeScript fundamentals — understanding type annotations, interfaces, classes, and generics in TypeScript
ES6+ features — using arrow functions, destructuring, spread operators, and other modern JavaScript features
npm scripts and package.json — managing dependencies, scripts, and metadata in a Node.js project
Testing frameworks — writing unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests using Jest, Mocha, or Cypress
Code organization and structure — organizing code into modules, folders, and files for maintainability and scalability
Version control with Git — understanding Git basics, committing changes, branching, merging, and resolving conflicts
API documentation and Swagger — generating API documentation using Swagger, JSDoc, or other tools

knowledge.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language used extensively in backend development due to its simplicity, readability, and extensive libraries. It's particularly popular for web development using frameworks like Django and Flask.
what_you_will_learn: Indentation — Python's unique syntax feature that uses whitespace to denote block-level structure
Variables and data types — understanding the different types (int, float, str, list, dict, set) and how to declare them
Control structures — learning about if-else statements, for loops, while loops, and break/continue statements
Functions — defining reusable blocks of code with arguments and return values
Modules and packages — importing and using external libraries like requests and pandas
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts — classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation
File input/output operations — reading and writing files using open() and close() functions
Exception handling — try-except blocks for error management
Regular expressions — pattern matching using re module
List comprehensions — concise syntax for creating lists from existing iterables
Dictionary comprehension — creating dictionaries from existing iterables
Set operations — union, intersection, difference using set() function
Lambda functions — small anonymous functions that can be passed as arguments to higher-order functions
Map, filter, reduce — applying functions to iterables using built-in functions
Decorators — modifying function behavior without changing the original code
Asyncio and concurrent.futures — asynchronous programming for I/O-bound tasks
Django framework basics — understanding views, templates, models, and forms
Flask framework basics — building web applications with microservices architecture
SQLAlchemy ORM — interacting with databases using object-relational mapping
Type hinting and type checking — using type annotations for static analysis and runtime checks
Async/await syntax — writing asynchronous code that's easier to read and maintain
Context managers — ensuring resources are released properly after use
Logging and debugging — using logging module for error tracking and pdb for step-through debugging

knowledge.what_it_is: Ruby is a high-level, dynamic programming language used for building scalable and maintainable web applications. It's known for its simplicity, readability, and ease of use, making it an ideal choice for backend development.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — declaring and using variables in Ruby, including local, instance, and class variables
Data Types — understanding Ruby's built-in data types, such as integers, floats, strings, arrays, hashes, and ranges
Control Structures — learning about if/else statements, loops (for, while, until), and conditional expressions
Methods — defining and calling methods in Ruby classes, including instance and class methods
Classes and Objects — creating and using classes to encapsulate data and behavior
Modules — importing and using modules to extend the functionality of classes
Blocks and Closures — understanding how blocks and closures work in Ruby, including lambda expressions
Hashes and Arrays — working with hashes and arrays, including iteration and manipulation
Regular Expressions — using regular expressions for pattern matching and string manipulation
File Input/Output — reading and writing files in Ruby, including binary I/O and file permissions
Networking — making HTTP requests and handling network connections in Ruby
Database Interactions — connecting to databases (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) and performing CRUD operations
Active Record Pattern — using the Active Record pattern for database interactions with Rails
MVC Architecture — understanding the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architecture and its implementation in Ruby on Rails
Rails Framework — learning about the Ruby on Rails framework, including routing, controllers, views, and helpers
Dependency Management — using Bundler to manage dependencies and gems in a Ruby project
Testing Frameworks — introducing RSpec and Minitest for unit testing and integration testing in Ruby
Security Best Practices — understanding common security risks and best practices for secure coding in Ruby
Error Handling — learning how to handle errors and exceptions in Ruby, including rescue blocks and error classes
Performance Optimization — techniques for optimizing performance in Ruby applications, including caching and memoization

knowledge.what_it_is: Java is an object-oriented, statically-typed programming language developed by Sun Microsystems (now owned by Oracle Corporation). It's widely used for building enterprise-level applications, Android apps, and web services due to its platform independence, robust security features, and vast ecosystem of libraries and tools.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — declaring and using variables in Java, including primitive types, reference types, and scoping
Data Types — understanding Java's built-in data types (int, double, boolean, char), as well as user-defined classes and interfaces
Operators — mastering arithmetic, comparison, logical, and assignment operators in Java
Control Flow Statements — using if-else statements, switch statements, loops (for, while, do-while), and jump statements (break, continue)
Methods — declaring and calling methods in Java, including overloaded and overridden methods
Constructors — understanding the role of constructors in initializing objects and creating new instances
Inheritance — learning about single inheritance, method overriding, and method overloading in Java
Polymorphism — mastering runtime polymorphism through method overriding and method overloading
Encapsulation — using access modifiers (public, private, protected) to control data access and hiding implementation details
Abstraction — understanding abstract classes and interfaces in Java for defining blueprints and contracts
Exception Handling — learning about try-catch blocks, exception types (checked vs. unchecked), and custom exceptions
File Input/Output — reading and writing files using FileInputStream, FileOutputStream, FileReader, and FileWriter
Networking — understanding sockets, URL connections, and HTTP requests in Java for building web services
Multithreading — learning about threads, locks, and synchronization mechanisms (synchronized blocks, ReentrantLock)
Java Collections Framework — mastering List, Set, Map interfaces, and their implementations (ArrayList, LinkedList, HashSet, HashMap)
Lambda Expressions — using lambda expressions to simplify code and improve readability
Method References — understanding method references for concise code and improved performance
Functional Interfaces — learning about functional interfaces and their use in Java 8 and later versions
Java 8 Features — mastering new features introduced in Java 8, including default methods, static methods, and type inference
Java 11+ Features — understanding new features added in Java 11 and later, such as var keyword, switch expressions, and pattern matching
Dependency Injection — using frameworks like Spring or Guice for dependency injection and loose coupling
Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) — learning about AOP concepts and their implementation in Java using libraries like AspectJ
Java 17 Features — mastering new features added in Java 17, including pattern matching for switch statements and sealed classes

knowledge.what_it_is: C# (pronounced 'C sharp') is a modern, object-oriented programming language developed by Microsoft as part of its .NET initiative. It is designed to work seamlessly with the .NET Framework and is widely used for building Windows desktop applications, web applications, and services.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — declaring and using variables in C#, including value types (int, bool) and reference types (string, object)
Data Types — understanding C#'s built-in data types (int, float, double, char), as well as nullable types and tuples
Operators — mastering C#'s operators for arithmetic, comparison, logical, and assignment operations
Control Flow Statements — using if-else statements, switch statements, loops (for, while, do-while), and jump statements (break, continue)
Methods — declaring and calling methods in C#, including method overloading and default parameters
Classes and Objects — understanding object-oriented programming concepts in C# (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism)
Inheritance and Polymorphism — using base classes, abstract classes, interfaces, and virtual methods to achieve code reuse
Arrays and Collections — working with arrays, lists (ArrayList, LinkedList), dictionaries (Dictionary), and sets
Exception Handling — catching and handling exceptions in C# using try-catch blocks and custom exception types
File Input/Output — reading and writing text files, binary files, and XML documents using streams and readers/writers
LINQ (Language Integrated Query) — querying data using LINQ's query syntax and extension methods
Async/Await — writing asynchronous code using async methods, await expressions, and Task objects
Multithreading and Parallelism — creating threads, tasks, and parallel loops to improve application performance
Attributes and Reflection — using attributes to decorate classes and methods, and reflecting on types and members at runtime
Serialization and Deserialization — serializing and deserializing data using BinaryFormatter, XmlSerializer, and JSON.NET
ASP.NET Core — building web applications using ASP.NET Core's Razor Pages, MVC, and API controllers
Entity Framework Core — working with databases using Entity Framework Core's DbContext, DbSet, and ChangeTracker
Dependency Injection — using dependency injection containers (Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection) to manage dependencies
Logging and Debugging — logging messages using NLog, Serilog, or Microsoft.Extensions.Logging, and debugging applications using Visual Studio
Unit Testing and Integration Testing — writing unit tests using xUnit, NUnit, or MSTest, and integration tests using API controllers and HTTP requests

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a free and open-source distributed version control system designed to handle large projects with speed and efficiency. It's widely used by backend developers to manage code changes, collaborate on projects, and maintain project history.
what_you_will_learn: Git repository structure — understanding the concept of .gitignore, .gitattributes, and .gitmodules
Initializing a Git repository — creating a new repository with git init, adding files with git add, and committing changes with git commit
Branching and merging — creating branches with git branch, switching between branches with git checkout, merging branches with git merge, and resolving conflicts
Git remote repositories — connecting to remote repos with git remote add, pushing changes with git push, and pulling changes with git pull
Git tags and releases — creating tags with git tag, creating releases with git release, and managing versioning
Git stash and rebase — using git stash to temporarily set aside changes and git rebase to integrate changes from other branches
Git diff and log — understanding the output of git diff and git log commands and how to use them for debugging
Git cherry-pick and revert — applying specific commits with git cherry-pick and reverting changes with git revert
Git bisect — using binary search to identify which commit introduced a bug or issue
Git hooks — understanding the different types of Git hooks (pre-commit, post-commit, etc.) and how to use them for automation

knowledge.what_it_is: GitLab is an open-source DevOps platform that provides a web-based interface for version control, issue tracking, and continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD). It supports various programming languages, including Java, Python, Ruby, and more. As a Backend Developer at Brain Station 23, you will use GitLab to manage your codebase, collaborate with team members, and automate testing and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: GitLab CI/CD — configuring automated pipelines for building, testing, and deploying applications
GitLab Runner — setting up and managing runners for executing jobs in the pipeline
GitLab API — interacting with GitLab using its REST API for automation and integration
GitLab Webhooks — receiving notifications from GitLab when specific events occur (e.g., push, merge, issue created)
GitLab Issues and Merge Requests — tracking bugs, feature requests, and code changes through the web interface
GitLab Code Review — reviewing and approving code changes using the web interface or API
GitLab Permissions and Access Control — managing user roles, permissions, and access to projects and repositories

knowledge.what_it_is: Bitbucket is a web-based source code repository manager hosted by Atlassian, designed to facilitate collaboration and version control among development teams.
what_you_will_learn: Repository creation — creating new repositories for projects using Bitbucket's UI or API
Branch management — understanding how to create, merge, and delete branches in Bitbucket
Pull requests — reviewing and merging code changes from other developers through pull requests
Code reviews — participating in code review processes, providing feedback on code quality and security
Permissions and access control — configuring user permissions, roles, and access levels for repositories
Webhooks and integrations — setting up webhooks to trigger automated tasks or integrating with other tools like Jenkins
Bitbucket Pipelines — using Bitbucket's built-in CI/CD tool to automate testing and deployment of code changes
Security features — understanding how Bitbucket enforces security measures such as two-factor authentication, SAML, and IP restrictions

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, widely used by developers and companies worldwide.
what_you_will_learn: Creating repositories — setting up new GitHub repos, configuring permissions, and managing access
Branching and merging — understanding Git flow, creating branches, committing changes, and resolving conflicts
Pull requests — submitting code changes for review, commenting on PRs, and approving or rejecting them
GitHub Actions — automating workflows with YAML scripts, integrating CI/CD pipelines, and deploying apps to production
Code reviews — best practices for reviewing code, providing feedback, and improving collaboration
Collaboration tools — using GitHub's built-in features like Issues, Projects, and Wiki for team communication and organization

knowledge.what_it_is: MySQL is an open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) widely used for web applications, created by Oracle Corporation. It supports ACID transactions, has a strong focus on SQL standards compliance, and offers high performance, scalability, and reliability.
what_you_will_learn: Database schema design — creating efficient table structures using primary keys, foreign keys, indexes, and normalization
SQL syntax basics — understanding SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements, subqueries, joins, and aggregations
Data types and storage — working with integer, string, date/time, and binary data types, including character sets and collations
Indexing and optimization — creating efficient indexes, using EXPLAIN to analyze query performance, and optimizing queries for better execution plans
Views and stored procedures — creating reusable views and stored procedures to encapsulate complex logic and improve maintainability
Transactions and locking — understanding transaction isolation levels, locks, and deadlocks, including row-level locking and table locking
Replication and high availability — setting up master-slave replication, using MySQL Cluster for high availability, and configuring asynchronous replication
Security and access control — managing user accounts, privileges, and roles, including password hashing and encryption
Backup and recovery — creating backups using mysqldump or xtrabackup, and restoring databases from backup files
Performance monitoring and tuning — using MySQL Workbench, Percona Toolkit, or other tools to monitor performance, identify bottlenecks, and optimize configuration
InnoDB storage engine basics — understanding InnoDB's architecture, including buffer pool, doublewrite buffer, and redo log
MyISAM storage engine basics — understanding MyISAM's architecture, including table locking and concurrent inserts
MySQL Workbench and command-line tools — using the MySQL Workbench GUI or command-line tools like mysql, mysqldump, and mysqlpump

knowledge.what_it_is: MariaDB is an open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) that supports ACID compliance, SQL syntax similar to MySQL, and a wide range of storage engines. It is highly customizable, scalable, and widely used in enterprise environments.
what_you_will_learn: SQL syntax — understanding MariaDB's SQL dialect and its differences from MySQL
Storage Engines — choosing between InnoDB, MyISAM, Aria, and other available storage engines for optimal performance
Table Partitioning — dividing large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces to improve query performance and reduce storage needs
Indexing Strategies — creating efficient indexes using B-tree, hash, and full-text indexing techniques
Views and Stored Procedures — defining reusable database logic with views and stored procedures for improved maintainability
Replication and Clustering — setting up master-slave replication and clustering configurations for high availability and scalability
Security Features — configuring user authentication, access control, and encryption to protect sensitive data

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL is an open-source, object-relational database management system (ORDBMS) that supports a wide range of data types and features advanced query capabilities. It is widely used in enterprise environments due to its reliability, scalability, and high-performance capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Data Types — PostgreSQL supports various data types such as integers, strings, dates, timestamps, and arrays
Tables and Schemas — understanding how to create, modify, and manage database tables and schemas
Constraints and Indexes — learning about primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, and indexes for optimizing query performance
SQL Syntax — mastering PostgreSQL's SQL syntax, including SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements
Views and Materialized Views — creating views to simplify complex queries and materialized views for improved performance
Triggers and Stored Procedures — understanding how to create triggers and stored procedures for automating database tasks
Transactions and Locking — learning about transaction isolation levels, locking mechanisms, and concurrency control
Query Optimization — techniques for optimizing PostgreSQL queries using indexes, caching, and query rewriting
Backup and Recovery — understanding how to backup and recover PostgreSQL databases using tools like pg_dump and pg_restore
Connection Pooling — implementing connection pooling using libraries like pgpool-II or pgbouncer
PostgreSQL Extensions — exploring extensions like PostGIS for geospatial data, PL/pgSQL for procedural programming, and pgcrypto for encryption
pg_stat_statements and Query Performance Monitoring — analyzing query performance using built-in tools like pg_stat_statements and external tools like New Relic
Security Best Practices — understanding how to secure PostgreSQL databases using features like SSL/TLS encryption, row-level security, and access control lists

knowledge.what_it_is: Microsoft SQL Server (MS SQL) is a relational database management system developed by Microsoft, widely used for enterprise-level applications due to its scalability, reliability, and high-performance capabilities. It supports various data types, indexing methods, and query optimization techniques.
what_you_will_learn: Database creation — understanding the CREATE DATABASE statement and database properties
Table structure — designing tables with primary keys, foreign keys, indexes, and constraints
Data types — mastering integer, string, date/time, and binary data types, including precision and scale for decimal numbers
Querying data — writing SELECT statements with filtering, sorting, grouping, and joining data from multiple tables
Stored procedures — creating and executing stored procedures to encapsulate complex logic and improve performance
Triggers — understanding trigger types (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) and using them to enforce business rules or audit changes
Views — creating and managing views to simplify complex queries and improve data security
Indexing — optimizing query performance with clustered and non-clustered indexes, including covering indexes and index hints

knowledge.what_it_is: Oracle is an enterprise-level relational database management system (RDBMS) developed by Oracle Corporation, widely used in large-scale applications and enterprises due to its robust security features, high performance, and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: SQL syntax — writing queries using SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements
Table structures — creating tables with columns, data types, constraints (primary keys, foreign keys)
Indexing — optimizing query performance by creating indexes on columns used in WHERE and JOIN clauses
Views — virtual tables based on the result of a SQL query, simplifying complex queries
Stored procedures — reusable blocks of code that perform specific tasks, improving database security
Triggers — automating actions before or after certain events (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) on tables
Sequence and synonym management — creating sequences for auto-incrementing IDs and synonyms for table aliases
Oracle SQL Developer — a graphical tool for designing, developing, and debugging Oracle databases
PL/SQL programming language — writing stored procedures, functions, and triggers in Oracle's procedural language extension
Oracle Data Guard — a high availability feature that replicates data across multiple sites for disaster recovery
Oracle RAC (Real Application Clusters) — a clustering technology for improving database performance and availability
Materialized views — pre-computed results of complex queries, reducing query execution time
Data encryption — securing sensitive data using Oracle's built-in encryption features
Row-level security — controlling access to specific rows in tables based on user permissions
Oracle Database 12c new features — understanding changes and improvements introduced in the latest version
Database partitioning — dividing large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces for improved performance
Oracle's Automatic Storage Management (ASM) — a storage management system that automates disk space allocation
Flashback database — recovering from logical corruption by restoring to a previous point-in-time
Data compression — reducing storage requirements and improving query performance using Oracle's compression features

knowledge.what_it_is: SQLite is a self-contained, file-based relational database management system (RDBMS) that allows storing and querying structured data using SQL. It's widely used in web applications due to its ease of use, small footprint, and zero-configuration setup.
what_you_will_learn: Creating and managing SQLite databases using the sqlite3 command-line tool or Python's sqlite3 module
Executing SQL queries: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements for data manipulation and retrieval
Understanding database schema design: creating tables, indexes, views, triggers, and constraints
Working with SQLite data types: INTEGER, REAL, TEXT, BLOB, NULL, and their interactions with SQL operators
Using SQLite's built-in functions: RANDOM(), IFNULL(), COALESCE(), and others for data manipulation and analysis
Implementing transactions and concurrency control in SQLite using SAVEPOINT, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK statements
Optimizing SQLite performance through indexing, caching, and query optimization techniques

knowledge.what_it_is: APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are sets of defined rules that enable different software systems to communicate with each other, allowing data exchange and integration between applications. In a backend context, APIs are used to expose services, resources, or business logic to external clients, such as web applications, mobile apps, or microservices.
what_you_will_learn: RESTful API design — designing APIs that follow the REST architectural style, using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), and resource-based URLs
API endpoints — defining specific entry points for clients to access resources or services, including query parameters and request bodies
HTTP methods — understanding the purpose of each HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) and how to use them in API design
Request and response formats — working with JSON, XML, and other data formats used for API communication, including serialization and deserialization
API security — implementing authentication and authorization mechanisms, such as OAuth, JWT, or Basic Auth, to protect APIs from unauthorized access
Error handling and logging — designing error responses that provide useful information to clients, and implementing logging mechanisms to monitor API usage and errors
API documentation — creating clear and concise documentation for APIs using tools like Swagger, OpenAPI, or API Blueprint
API testing — writing tests for APIs using frameworks like Postman, cURL, or Pytest, to ensure correct behavior and catch bugs early
API caching — implementing caching mechanisms, such as Redis or Memcached, to improve API performance and reduce latency

knowledge.what_it_is: HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) is an architectural style that emphasizes resource discovery and manipulation through hyperlinks embedded within the application's API responses. This approach enables clients to navigate and interact with the API without prior knowledge of its structure or endpoints.
what_you_will_learn: Resource representation — understanding how HATEOAS APIs represent resources as JSON objects, including metadata and links
Hypermedia controls — learning about the various types of hyperlinks (e.g., rel, href, media type) used to convey application state and actions
Link relations — studying common link relation types (e.g., self, next, prev, up) and their usage in HATEOAS APIs
Client-driven navigation — understanding how clients use hypermedia controls to navigate the API and discover new resources
Server-driven pagination — learning about techniques for implementing server-driven pagination using HATEOAS links
API discovery — studying strategies for discovering available API endpoints, methods, and parameters through HATEOAS links
Error handling — understanding how HATEOAS APIs handle errors and provide feedback to clients through hypermedia controls
validation_keywords: hypermedia as the engine of application state

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenAPI Specification (OAS) is an open-source, vendor-agnostic standard for describing RESTful APIs. It provides a common language for both humans and machines to understand API documentation, making it easier to design, build, and consume APIs.
what_you_will_learn: API Design — defining API endpoints, methods, parameters, and responses using OAS
Swagger UI — generating interactive API documentation with code samples and try-it-out functionality
OpenAPI Schema — defining data types, formats, and validation rules for API requests and responses
API Security — integrating security features like OAuth2, JWT, and SSL/TLS into OAS definitions
Serverless APIs — designing APIs that run on serverless platforms like AWS Lambda or Google Cloud Functions
API Gateways — configuring API gateways like NGINX, Amazon API Gateway, or Google Cloud Endpoints to manage API traffic
API Versioning — implementing versioning strategies like URI-based, header-based, or query parameter-based versioning in OAS

knowledge.what_it_is: Representational State of Resource (REST) is an architectural style for designing networked applications, emphasizing simplicity and modularity. It's a widely adopted standard for building scalable web services, used by major companies like Amazon, Google, and Netflix.
what_you_will_learn: Resource identification — assigning unique identifiers to resources using URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers)
Resource manipulation — using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to perform CRUD operations on resources
Client-server architecture — separating concerns between client and server, with the client making requests and the server handling state changes
Statelessness — servers not maintaining session information or application state across requests
Cacheability — using HTTP headers (Cache-Control, ETag) to control caching behavior and reduce unnecessary requests
Uniform interface — defining a standard set of operations and data formats for interacting with resources
Layered system — breaking down complex systems into smaller, independent layers, each responsible for a specific function

knowledge.what_it_is: JSON APIs are web services that use JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) as the primary data format for exchanging data between client and server. They are commonly used in RESTful architectures, where requests and responses are formatted using HTTP methods and JSON payload.
what_you_will_learn: RESTful API design — designing APIs that follow the principles of REST (Resource, Representation, State, Transfer), including URI structure, HTTP method usage, and response formats
JSON data structures — understanding how to represent complex data in JSON format, including arrays, objects, and nested structures
JSON serialization and deserialization — using libraries like Jackson or Gson for converting Java objects to JSON and vice versa
API endpoint design — designing API endpoints that are intuitive, consistent, and follow RESTful principles
Error handling and responses — understanding how to handle errors and return meaningful responses in JSON format, including HTTP status codes and error messages
JSON Schema validation — using JSON Schema to validate incoming requests and ensure data consistency
API security measures — implementing security measures like authentication, authorization, and rate limiting for protecting API endpoints

knowledge.what_it_is: SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) is an XML-based messaging protocol used for exchanging structured information between systems, typically used for web services and enterprise integration. It's a widely adopted standard for building interoperable APIs.
what_you_will_learn: SOAP Request/Response — Understanding the basic structure of SOAP messages, including the Envelope, Header, Body, and Fault elements
SOAP Methods — Learning about the different HTTP methods used with SOAP (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and their implications for API design
WSDL (Web Services Description Language) — Creating and consuming WSDL files to describe SOAP services, including service endpoints and operations
SOAP Faults — Handling errors and exceptions in SOAP messages, including fault codes and descriptions
Apache Axis2 — Understanding the popular open-source SOAP engine for Java, including its features and configuration options
WS-Security (Web Services Security) — Implementing security measures for SOAP web services, including authentication, authorization, and encryption
SOAP Attachments — Working with binary data in SOAP messages, including file uploads and downloads

knowledge.what_it_is: gRPC is a high-performance RPC framework developed by Google, designed to enable efficient and scalable communication between microservices. It supports multiple programming languages, including Java, Python, C++, and Go.
what_you_will_learn: Protocol Buffers — gRPC's message format for serializing structured data, replacing JSON or XML
gRPC Services — defining APIs using the gRPC service definition language (SDL), with methods and request/response types
gRPC Channels — establishing connections between clients and servers, supporting HTTP/2, WebSockets, and TCP
Unary RPCs — sending requests and receiving responses in a single call, suitable for simple operations
Server Streaming RPCs — sending multiple responses from the server to the client in a single request
Client Streaming RPCs — sending multiple requests from the client to the server in a single call
Bidirectional Streaming RPCs — simultaneous communication between client and server, ideal for real-time data exchange
gRPC Load Balancing — distributing incoming traffic across multiple instances of a service for high availability

knowledge.what_it_is: GraphQL is a query language for APIs, created by Facebook (now Meta), that allows clients to specify exactly what data they need and receive only that data in response. It's designed to be more efficient than traditional RESTful APIs and has become increasingly popular due to its flexibility and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: Schema Definition Language (SDL) — defining the schema of a GraphQL API using a specific syntax
Query Resolvers — functions that fetch data from various sources, such as databases or external APIs, in response to GraphQL queries
Mutation Resolvers — functions that modify data in a GraphQL API, often used for CRUD operations
Schema Stitching — combining multiple GraphQL schemas into a single schema, allowing for more complex and scalable APIs
Type System — defining the types of fields and arguments in a GraphQL schema, including scalar types, object types, and interface types
Input Types — specifying the structure of data sent to a GraphQL API through mutations or queries
Subscriptions — real-time updates pushed from a server to clients over WebSockets or other transports

knowledge.what_it_is: Server-side programming refers to the execution of code on a remote server, providing services and data to clients over a network. In the context of backend development, server-side programming involves writing code that runs on a web server, handling requests, processing data, and returning responses.
what_you_will_learn: Node.js — a JavaScript runtime environment for building scalable server-side applications using asynchronous I/O
Express.js — a popular Node.js framework for building web applications, providing routing, middleware, and template engines
Koa.js — an enterprise-level Node.js framework offering built-in support for async/await, middleware composition, and error handling
Serverless computing with AWS Lambda or Google Cloud Functions — deploying code without provisioning servers, using event-driven architecture
API Gateway — a managed service providing a single entry point for clients to access APIs, handling authentication, rate limiting, and caching
Load Balancing with HAProxy or NGINX — distributing incoming traffic across multiple servers to improve responsiveness and reliability
Database Connection Pools — managing connections to relational databases like MySQL or PostgreSQL using connection pooling libraries like pool2 or pgbouncer

knowledge.what_it_is: Memcached is an open-source, distributed memory object caching system that stores data in RAM to reduce the number of queries made to a database or other storage systems. It's commonly used by web applications to improve performance and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: Installing Memcached on Linux/Unix-based systems — using package managers like apt-get, yum, or homebrew
Configuring Memcached for optimal performance — tuning cache size, memory usage, and other settings
Using Memcached with PHP — integrating it with popular frameworks like Laravel and CodeIgniter
Implementing caching strategies in Memcached — including cache expiration, cache invalidation, and cache clustering
Memcached data types — understanding the difference between string, binary, and hash types
Memcached operations — learning about get, set, delete, increment, decrement, and other basic commands
Using Memcached with other caching systems — integrating it with Redis, APC, and other popular caching solutions

knowledge.what_it_is: A Content Delivery Network (CDN) is a distributed network of servers that cache and serve static content, such as images, videos, and stylesheets, to reduce latency and improve page load times. CDNs are commonly used by web applications to optimize performance and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: CDN types — understanding the differences between cloud-based (e.g., Cloudflare) and edge-based (e.g., Akamai) CDNs
Origin Pull vs Push CDN — configuring your origin server to serve content through a CDN, or pushing content directly from the CDN
Cache Control headers — using HTTP headers to control how long cached content is stored on the client-side
CDN caching strategies — understanding how CDNs cache and serve content, including cache expiration, invalidation, and purging
SSL/TLS termination at the edge — configuring your CDN to handle SSL/TLS encryption for secure connections
IP blocking and rate limiting — using CDNs to block malicious traffic and prevent DDoS attacks

knowledge.what_it_is: Client-side refers to the part of a web application that runs on the client's browser, responsible for rendering and interacting with the user interface.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP Request Handling — understanding how to handle HTTP requests from clients using frameworks like Express.js or Flask
Cookie Management — learning about cookie-based authentication, session management, and secure storage of sensitive data
WebSockets — implementing real-time communication between client and server using WebSocket protocols
Client-Side Validation — writing JavaScript code to validate user input on the client-side before sending it to the server
AJAX Requests — making asynchronous HTTP requests from JavaScript code to fetch or send data to the server
JSON Data Format — understanding how to work with JSON data, including parsing, serialization, and deserialization
Client-Side Caching — implementing caching mechanisms on the client-side using libraries like Redis or Memcached

knowledge.what_it_is: Basic Authentication is a simple authentication mechanism used to verify user credentials, typically sent over HTTP as Base64-encoded username and password pairs.
what_you_will_learn: HTTP Basic Auth — understanding how client sends credentials with each request, and server verifies them
Authorization Header — learning about the format of the Authorization header, including scheme, credentials, and realm
Base64 Encoding — decoding Base64-encoded strings to extract username and password
User Credentials Storage — securely storing user passwords using hashing algorithms like bcrypt or PBKDF2
Authentication Flow — understanding the sequence of events in a basic auth request-response cycle
401 Unauthorized Response — learning about the HTTP status code for authentication failures and how to handle it
Basic Auth with HTTPS — understanding how Basic Auth works over secure connections (HTTPS)
Caching Authentication Results — optimizing performance by caching authentication results, such as user roles or permissions

knowledge.what_it_is: Token authentication is an authorization mechanism where a server issues a token to clients, which they use to authenticate subsequent requests. This approach is widely used in web APIs and microservices.
what_you_will_learn: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) — digitally signed tokens containing user claims, commonly used for stateless authentication
Token-based authentication vs session-based authentication: understanding the trade-offs between token expiration, revocation, and storage
Token formats: JWT, SAML, OAuth access tokens, and custom token formats using libraries like PyJWT or jsonwebtoken
Token validation: verifying token signatures, checking token expiration, and handling token blacklisting
Token generation: creating tokens using secret keys, algorithms (HMAC SHA256), and claims (user ID, roles, permissions)
Refresh tokens: understanding the concept of refresh tokens for long-lived sessions and token renewal
Token storage: secure storage mechanisms for tokens in databases, Redis, or Memcached

knowledge.what_it_is: Cookie-based authentication is a method of verifying user identity by storing and retrieving authentication data in HTTP cookies, typically used for session management.
what_you_will_learn: HTTPOnly Cookies — setting cookies to prevent JavaScript access and tampering
Secure Cookies — marking cookies as secure to ensure they're transmitted over HTTPS only
Cookie Flags — understanding the Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, and Path flags for cookie management
Session Management — using cookies to store and manage user sessions, including session IDs and timeouts
CSRF Protection — implementing CSRF tokens in cookies to prevent cross-site request forgery attacks
Cookie-Based Authentication Flow — understanding the steps involved in authenticating users with cookies, from login to logout

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenID Connect (OIDC) is an authentication protocol built on top of the OAuth 2.0 authorization framework, allowing clients to securely authenticate users and obtain their identity information.
what_you_will_learn: OpenID Connect flows — understanding the authorization code flow, implicit flow, hybrid flow, and client credentials flow
Client registration — registering a client with an OIDC provider, including obtaining a client ID and secret
Authorization endpoint — sending a request to the authorization endpoint to initiate authentication
Token endpoint — exchanging authorization codes for access tokens at the token endpoint
ID token validation — verifying the contents of an ID token, including checking its signature and claims
Scope and claims — understanding how scopes and claims are used in OIDC to restrict user data exposure
OpenID Connect discovery — using the .well-known/openid-configuration endpoint to discover OIDC provider metadata
PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) — implementing PKCE to prevent authorization code interception attacks

knowledge.what_it_is: SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language) is an XML-based standard for exchanging authentication and authorization data between systems, commonly used for single sign-on (SSO) and identity federation. It's widely adopted by major enterprises and organizations worldwide.
what_you_will_learn: SAML protocol — understanding the SAML request/response flow, including AuthNRequest, AuthnResponse, LogoutRequest, and LogoutResponse
SAML assertions — learning about assertion types (AuthnStatement, AttrStatement), assertion conditions, and assertion attributes
SAML bindings — studying the different binding protocols (HTTP-POST, HTTP-Redirect, SOAP), their use cases, and limitations
SAML identity providers (IdPs) — configuring IdPs like Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, and Shibboleth for SSO with your application
SAML service providers (SPs) — setting up SPs to consume SAML assertions from IdPs, including configuration of ACS URLs and logout URLs
SAML metadata — understanding the importance of metadata files in SAML configurations, including entity IDs, assertion consumer services, and single sign-on services
SAML attribute mapping — learning how to map user attributes between your application and the IdP, including name ID formats and attribute names
SAML logout — implementing SAML logout functionality, including session invalidation and redirecting users back to the IdP

knowledge.what_it_is: Authentication refers to the process of verifying the identity and integrity of users, devices, or systems before granting access to a system, application, or resource. In the context of backend development, authentication is crucial for securing APIs, web applications, and other services.
what_you_will_learn: OAuth 2.0 — an authorization framework that allows clients to obtain limited-access tokens to access protected resources
OpenID Connect (OIDC) — a simple identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 for authentication and user information exchange
JSON Web Tokens (JWT) — a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims between two parties
Session Management — techniques for storing and managing user session data, including cookie-based sessions and token-based sessions
Password Hashing — algorithms and libraries used to securely store passwords, such as bcrypt, Argon2, and PBKDF2
Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) — methods of requiring users to provide a second form of verification in addition to their password
Single Sign-On (SSO) — systems that allow users to access multiple applications or services with a single set of credentials

knowledge.what_it_is: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) are a compact, URL-safe means of securely transmitting information between parties as a JSON object. They are digitally signed and contain payload data that can be verified by any party with the secret key.
what_you_will_learn: Token structure — understanding the three parts of a JWT: header, payload, and signature
Signing algorithms — learning about popular signing algorithms like HS256, RS256, and ES256
Key management — managing private keys securely using tools like OpenSSL or Hashicorp's Vault
Payload claims — understanding standard claims like iss, sub, aud, exp, and iat, as well as custom claims
Token verification — implementing token verification in a backend application using libraries like PyJWT or jsonwebtoken
Blacklisting tokens — learning about token blacklisting techniques to prevent token reuse attacks
Refresh tokens — understanding the concept of refresh tokens for long-lived sessions

knowledge.what_it_is: OAuth is an authorization framework that allows clients to access resources on behalf of users without sharing sensitive credentials, commonly used for web and mobile applications.
what_you_will_learn: Authorization Code Flow — obtaining an access token by redirecting the user to the authorization server with a code
Implicit Flow — obtaining an access token directly from the authorization server without a code, typically used for clients that cannot store secrets securely
Client Credentials Flow — obtaining an access token using client credentials (client ID and secret) for server-to-server interactions
Resource Owner Password Credentials Flow — obtaining an access token by exchanging user credentials (username and password) directly with the authorization server
Refresh Tokens — refreshing expired access tokens to obtain new ones without requiring re-authentication
Scope and Permissions — defining and managing permissions for clients to access specific resources or scopes within an application
Token Introspection — verifying the validity of an access token by querying the authorization server
JSON Web Tokens (JWT) — using JWT as a compact, URL-safe means of representing claims between two parties

knowledge.what_it_is: MD5 (Message-Digest Algorithm 5) is a widely used cryptographic hash function that produces a 128-bit (16-byte) hash value, commonly represented as a hexadecimal string. It is designed to be fast and has been widely adopted for various purposes, including password storage, data integrity checks, and digital signatures.
what_you_will_learn: Hash functions — understanding the purpose of MD5 in cryptographic applications
MD5 algorithm — learning how MD5 produces a fixed-size hash value from variable-sized input data
Collision resistance — recognizing the limitations of MD5 due to its collision vulnerability
Preimage resistance — understanding why MD5 is not suitable for password storage and digital signatures
Hash length extension attack — learning about a specific attack that exploits MD5's weakness in generating hash values
MD5 implementation in programming languages (e.g., Python, Java) — understanding how to use MD5 libraries and functions
Secure hash functions alternatives (e.g., SHA-256, BLAKE2) — recognizing the need for more secure hash functions

knowledge.what_it_is: SHA (Secure Hash Algorithm) is a cryptographic hash function used to create a digital fingerprint of data, ensuring its integrity and authenticity. In the context of web development, SHA is commonly used for password storage and verification.
what_you_will_learn: SHA-256 — the most widely used variant of SHA, producing a 256-bit (32-byte) hash value
SHA-1 vs SHA-2 — understanding the differences between these two variants and their respective vulnerabilities
Hashing algorithms for password storage — using SHA to store passwords securely in databases
Password verification with SHA — implementing secure password verification using hashed values
Preventing rainbow table attacks — using salted hashes and other countermeasures to protect against brute-force attacks
Using libraries like bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 for password hashing — understanding the trade-offs between these alternatives

knowledge.what_it_is: OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project) Risks are a comprehensive list of common web application security risks, including injection attacks, cross-site scripting (XSS), and more. As a backend developer, understanding these risks is crucial to building secure applications.
what_you_will_learn: SQL Injection — injecting malicious SQL code into database queries to extract or modify sensitive data
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — injecting client-side scripts into web pages to steal user data or take control of user sessions
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) — tricking users into performing unintended actions on a web application
Command Injection — injecting operating system commands into database queries or other applications
Path Traversal — accessing and manipulating files outside the intended directory structure
Data Validation — ensuring user input is properly sanitized to prevent injection attacks
Error Handling — implementing secure error handling mechanisms to prevent information disclosure
Authentication Bypass — exploiting vulnerabilities in authentication mechanisms to gain unauthorized access

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) is a secure communication protocol used to establish encrypted connections between web servers and clients, ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data. It is built on top of TLS (Transport Layer Security), which provides end-to-end encryption.
what_you_will_learn: Certificate Authority (CA) — trusted third-party organizations that issue digital certificates for HTTPS
Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) — a framework for managing public-private key pairs and digital certificates
TLS Handshake — the process of establishing an encrypted connection between client and server, including certificate verification and key exchange
SSL/TLS Protocol Versions — understanding the differences between SSL 2.0/3.0 and TLS 1.0-1.3, including deprecated versions and supported ciphers
Certificate Pinning — securely binding a digital certificate to an application or service to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks
HTTPS Redirects — implementing redirects from HTTP to HTTPS using server-side configurations (e.g., Apache, Nginx)
OCSP Stapling — improving performance by reducing the number of OCSP requests and minimizing latency
Certificate Transparency (CT) — a framework for monitoring and auditing digital certificates to prevent tampering and misuse

knowledge.what_it_is: SSL/TLS (Secure Sockets Layer/Transport Layer Security) is a cryptographic protocol used to secure communication between a web server and clients, ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data. It's essential for backend developers to understand SSL/TLS as it's widely adopted in the industry, particularly with the shift towards HTTPS.
what_you_will_learn: Certificate Authority (CA) — an intermediate entity that issues digital certificates to verify identity and trust
Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) — a system for managing public-private key pairs and digital certificates
SSL/TLS Handshake — the process of establishing a secure connection between client and server, involving certificate verification and key exchange
Certificate Verification — checking the validity and authenticity of a digital certificate using algorithms like OCSP and CRL
Cipher Suites — combinations of cryptographic algorithms used for encryption and decryption, such as AES-GCM and ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256
Certificate Pinning — storing and verifying the expected certificate hash to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks
SSL/TLS Configuration — setting up SSL/TLS on web servers using tools like OpenSSL, Nginx, or Apache
HTTPS Redirects — automatically redirecting HTTP traffic to HTTPS using server-side redirects or client-side JavaScript

knowledge.what_it_is: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is an HTTP header-based security feature that allows web servers to relax the restrictions imposed by the same-origin policy, enabling web applications to make requests to resources from different domains.
what_you_will_learn: Origin — understanding how the browser identifies the origin of a request and how it affects CORS
Access-Control-Allow-Origin (ACAO) — configuring server-side headers to allow cross-origin requests
Preflight Requests — understanding the OPTIONS method used by browsers to preflight CORS requests
Simple vs. Preflighted Requests — learning when each type of request is made and how to handle them
CORS Policy — defining a policy for handling CORS requests, including allowed origins, methods, and headers
Expose-Headers — specifying which response headers should be exposed to the client
Max Age — setting the maximum age of a CORS configuration
Credentials — understanding when credentials are sent with cross-origin requests

knowledge.what_it_is: Content Security Policy (CSP) is a security feature implemented in web browsers that helps prevent cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks by defining which sources of content are allowed to be executed within a web page. It's a crucial security measure for backend developers to implement and configure correctly, as it can help protect against common web vulnerabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Policy creation — understanding how to create and define policies using the Content-Security-Policy HTTP header or meta tag
Policy directives — learning about specific policy directives such as 'default-src', 'script-src', 'style-src', and 'connect-src' and their usage scenarios
Source specification — understanding how to specify sources for different types of content, including scripts, stylesheets, images, and fonts
Nonce generation — learning about the importance of generating a unique nonce (number used once) for each page load to prevent XSS attacks
Policy reporting — understanding how browsers report CSP violations and how to use this information to improve policy configuration
CSP upgrade-insecure-requests — learning about this directive that helps migrate from insecure protocols to secure ones, such as HTTPS
Frame-ancestors policy — understanding the importance of specifying which domains can embed your web page in an iframe

knowledge.what_it_is: Server security refers to the measures taken to protect web servers from unauthorized access, data breaches, and other cyber threats. It involves implementing secure protocols, configuring server settings, and monitoring for potential vulnerabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Firewall configuration — setting up network firewalls to control incoming and outgoing traffic
Secure protocol implementation — using HTTPS, TLS, and SSL/TLS to encrypt data in transit
Authentication and authorization — implementing user authentication (LDAP, OAuth, JWT) and role-based access control (RBAC)
Password storage and management — securely storing passwords with bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2
Input validation and sanitization — preventing SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks
Regular security updates and patches — keeping server software up-to-date to prevent exploitation of known vulnerabilities
Intrusion detection and prevention systems (IDPS) — monitoring network traffic for signs of unauthorized access or malicious activity
Secure file permissions and ownership — configuring file system settings to limit access to sensitive data
Logging and auditing — implementing logging mechanisms to track server activity and detect potential security incidents

knowledge.what_it_is: Bcrypt is a widely used, open-source password hashing algorithm designed to protect against brute-force attacks and rainbow table attacks. It is implemented in many programming languages, including Java, Python, and Node.js.
what_you_will_learn: Hashing vs salting — understanding the difference between hashing and salting, and why bcrypt uses both
Bcrypt algorithm — learning how bcrypt generates a hash from a password using a salt value and multiple iterations of hashing
Cost factor (work factor) — configuring the cost factor to balance security and performance trade-offs
Password stretching — understanding how bcrypt slows down attackers by requiring significant computational resources
Salt management — learning how to generate, store, and manage unique salt values for each user
Bcrypt implementation in Node.js — using libraries like bcryptjs or bcrypt-nodejs to integrate bcrypt into a Node.js application
Error handling — understanding how to handle errors that may occur during the hashing process

knowledge.what_it_is: Scrypt is a password-based key derivation function (PBKDF) designed to be computationally expensive and resistant to side-channel attacks, making it suitable for password storage and authentication.
what_you_will_learn: Key derivation — the process of generating a unique key from a password or passphrase using scrypt
Salted hash — adding a random salt value to the input data before hashing with scrypt
Scrypt parameters — understanding the impact of N, r, and p on computational cost and security
Memory-hard functions — how scrypt's memory-intensive design resists side-channel attacks
Collision resistance — ensuring that different inputs produce distinct outputs when hashed with scrypt
Preimage resistance — preventing an attacker from finding a preimage (original input) given the hash output
Scrypt variants — understanding the differences between scrypt and other PBKDFs like Argon2 and Bcrypt

knowledge.what_it_is: Integration testing is a software testing technique where individual units of source code are combined and tested to ensure they function as expected when working together.
what_you_will_learn: API Gateway — integrating multiple microservices behind a single entry point for external requests
Mocking frameworks (e.g. Mockito, Pytest-Mock) — creating mock objects to isolate dependencies during testing
Dependency injection — decoupling components by providing dependencies through constructors or setters
Service discovery (e.g. Netflix Eureka, Apache ZooKeeper) — enabling services to discover and communicate with each other at runtime
API mocking tools (e.g. Postman, Apigee Mock API) — simulating external APIs for testing purposes
Containerization (e.g. Docker) — packaging applications and their dependencies into containers for consistent testing environments
Testcontainers (e.g. Testcontainers-Java, Testcontainers-Python) — running containerized tests with minimal setup
API security testing (e.g. OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite) — identifying vulnerabilities in API endpoints and data flows
Load testing tools (e.g. Apache JMeter, Gatling) — simulating large workloads to ensure system scalability
Performance monitoring (e.g. Prometheus, Grafana) — tracking key performance indicators for optimized system behavior

knowledge.what_it_is: Unit testing is a software development practice where individual units of source code, such as functions or methods, are tested to ensure they behave as expected. In the context of backend development, unit testing helps catch bugs early on and ensures that each component works correctly before integrating it with other parts of the system.
what_you_will_learn: Test-Driven Development (TDD) — writing tests before implementing code, driving development through test cases
Unit Testing Frameworks: Pytest, Unittest, Behave — using libraries to write and run unit tests in Python
Mocking libraries: Mockk, Mockito, Pytest-mock — isolating dependencies for unit testing by mocking external services or objects
Assertion Libraries: Assert, Should, Pytest-assertions — verifying expected behavior through assertions
Test Fixtures: setup/teardown methods, context managers — managing test environment and resources
Parameterized Testing: Pytest.mark.parametrize, Unittest parameterize — testing multiple inputs with a single test function
Property-Based Testing: Hypothesis, QuickCheck — testing functions based on their properties rather than specific inputs
Unit Test Structure: Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern — organizing unit tests using this structure
Test Coverage Metrics: Code coverage tools like Pytest-cov, Unittest-coverage — measuring test effectiveness and code coverage

knowledge.what_it_is: Functional testing is a software testing technique that ensures an application's functionality meets the requirements by verifying its behavior under various scenarios, including happy paths and edge cases.
what_you_will_learn: Test-Driven Development (TDD) — writing automated tests before implementing code to ensure it works as expected
Unit Testing — isolating individual units of code to test their functionality in isolation
Mocking and Stubbing — replacing dependencies with mock objects or stubs to isolate the unit under test
Pytest and Unittest — using popular Python testing frameworks for writing and running tests
Test Fixtures — setting up and tearing down resources required for testing, such as databases and file systems
Parameterized Testing — testing multiple inputs and scenarios with a single test function
Property-Based Testing — using libraries like Hypothesis to generate test cases based on properties of the code
API Testing with Requests and Pytest-requests — verifying API endpoints work correctly
Database Testing with pytest-sqlalchemy — testing database interactions and migrations
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) Pipelines — automating testing and deployment processes

knowledge.what_it_is: Object-Relational Mappers (ORMs) are software libraries that enable developers to interact with relational databases using high-level, programming language-specific APIs. They abstract away low-level database operations and provide a more intuitive way of working with data.
what_you_will_learn: Entity Framework Core — a popular ORM for .NET developers, providing a rich set of features for database modeling and querying
NHibernate — an open-source ORM for .NET, offering advanced features like caching and lazy loading
Hibernate — a Java-based ORM that provides a simple and efficient way to interact with databases
SQLAlchemy — a Python ORM that supports both synchronous and asynchronous database operations
Doctrine — a PHP ORM that offers a wide range of features, including support for multiple database engines
JPA (Java Persistence API) — a standard ORM interface for Java developers, providing a common way to interact with databases
Active Record Pattern — an ORM design pattern that allows developers to treat database tables as objects in their code
Repository Pattern — an ORM design pattern that separates data access logic from business logic

knowledge.what_it_is: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is a set of principles that ensure database transactions are processed reliably and securely. It's a fundamental concept in database design and management, crucial for maintaining data integrity in distributed systems.
